summary: This interview with Epsio CEO Gilad Kleinman explores incremental view maintenance (IVM) and streaming SQL technologies. Kleinman explains how streaming SQL differs from traditional batch processing by incrementally updating query results rather than reprocessing entire datasets. Epsio's approach is distinctive in that it connects to existing PostgreSQL/MySQL databases via CDC, processes changes externally, and writes materialized results back to the source database. The conversation covers how IVM fits into the broader landscape of streaming databases versus stream processors, the technical approaches behind differential dataflow and DBSP, and why IVM is expanding beyond traditional streaming use cases into customer-facing analytics and real-time reporting.
key_points: Streaming SQL processes changes incrementally rather than reprocessing entire datasets, enabling orders of magnitude faster and cheaper query results for predefined queries

Epsio differentiates by augmenting existing PostgreSQL/MySQL deployments rather than requiring migration to a new streaming database, consuming CDC streams and writing results back to the original database
The consolidation mechanism in streaming SQL prevents overwhelming source databases by batching multiple changes into single updates rather than writing each change individually

IVM use cases are shifting from traditional streaming scenarios like fraud detection toward customer-facing analytics, data transformations, and real-time reporting where query performance and cost savings are the primary drivers
Both differential dataflow and DBSP enable highly parallel processing with strong consistency guarantees and support for iterative/recursive queries that older stream processors lack
money_quotes: No matter how fast or efficient a traditional database is, if you are running a heavy query and most of the dataset hasn't changed since the last run, there is a lot of wasted compute.

By sitting 'behind' the existing database, reading CDC logs, and writing back results to the original database, users can integrate Epsio without worrying about affecting anything other than the results tables it needs to maintain.

We've seen companies make customer-facing analytics 100x faster, drop infrastructure costs by an order of magnitude, and turn reports that took hours to execute into ones that complete in under a second.

Adopting new database technologies is a pretty scary endeavor. We found that asking companies to install a new extension (that could potentially crash) on their production database was a pretty big ask to make.

I believe IVMs offer a pretty fundamental change in how organizations can work with their data—building data structures around specific queries vs. trying to store data to be 'generically' fast for any query.
Doing streaming SQL at scale for real-world use cases is no easy task.
key_insight: Incremental view maintenance is evolving from niche streaming use cases to become a fundamental approach for delivering fast, cost-effective analytics by building query-specific data structures rather than optimizing for generic query patterns.
